observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review to ensure only items meeting the ₱50,000.00 threshold are recorded under PPE accounts, while items below this threshold are reclassified as inventory or expensed upon issuance to end users.
Implement updated classification procedures by establishing a clear process for classifying assets based on the new threshold to ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 2024-006.
Reclassify the ₱1,033,698.06 worth of items from the PPE accounts to the correct semi-expendable inventory accounts and recognize them as semi-expendable expenses upon issuance to end users.
Establish a periodic reconciliation process to monitor and ensure that only eligible items are recorded under PPE accounts, while non-qualifying items are accurately tracked as inventory and expenses.
Prepare necessary adjusting journal entries to recognize the issuance to end-users of these semi-expendable property.
Improve the cost tracking and documentation by implementing a more robust and detailed system for tracking project costs. This will help ensure that project expenses are recorded accurately and will facilitate proper allocation and reclassification when projects are completed.
Immediately reclassify all completed projects from the CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts.
Conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE to reconcile discrepancies between accounting and general services records.
Create an appraisal committee to determine the value of these PPE.
Review the PPE acquisitions for Calendar Year (CY) 2024 and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to recognize the corresponding depreciation expense for these assets in accordance with applicable accounting standard.
We further recommended the following adjusting journal entries:
To record the reclassification of PPE to semi-expendable property
e. To record the issuance of semi-expendable property

We recommended that the management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to review the identified discrepancies and prepare the appropriate adjusting entries to rectify the issues mentioned above.
Conduct a thorough review and monitor the utilization of the LDRRM Fund to ensure compliance with the provisions of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, regarding the transfer of unutilized LDRRM Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM Fund, and
Discontinue the practice of reporting the transfer of the LDRRM Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM account as utilization of the DRRM Fund.
We further recommended the following adjusting journal entries:
To recognize the reclassification of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment to Semi-Expendable property
To record the issuance of semi-expendable property
To recognize the transfer of unexpended Quick Response and Mitigation Fund to Trust Liability-DRRM Account
To recognize the utilization of prior years’ unexpended quick response and mitigation fund and to record the reversal of cost of rice (relief for typhoon Nika)

To further enhance the efficiency and transparency of fund utilization, we recommend that management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to take the necessary steps to transfer the unexpended/unutilized LDRRMF for CY 2024 to a special trust fund account.
Direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer, and Local DRRMO to coordinate and assess the implementation and utilization status of LDRRM programs to ensure accurate and timely reporting on the use and progress of the LDRRM Fund.

We recommend that Management take the following actions:
Direct all implementing offices to conduct a thorough reassessment of all PPAs under continuing appropriations as of CY 2024 to determine appropriate disposition and relevance.
Formally declare savings from completed or discontinued projects and ensure timely reversion to the General Fund. These reverted funds should then be subjected to supplemental budgeting in support of other priority programs of the municipality.
Require regular monitoring and status reporting of PPAs from concerned offices. These reports should detail implementation progress, including issues and delays, and be submitted periodically to the Local Chief Executive, with a copy furnished to the Commission on Audit (COA).

We recommend that the management:
Strictly enforce the adoption and implementation of the Perpetual Inventory System in accordance with Chapter 7 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs to ensure continuous and accurate monitoring of inventory movements.
Require the General Services Office (GSO) to prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) – Annex 35 of NGAS manual, regularly to the Accounting Office to facilitate timely and correct recording of supplies consumption and inventory updates.
Direct the Accounting Office to record inventory transactions using the Moving Average Method and conduct regular reconciliations between accounting records and actual physical inventory to detect discrepancies promptly.
Institutionalize inter-office coordination mechanisms such as periodic meetings, inventory review sessions, and a shared inventory monitoring system between the GSO, Accounting Office, and other relevant departments to promote accuracy, accountability, and timely reporting of inventory transactions.

We recommend that the management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the records of the municipality with the records of the GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth, and
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to remit all prior period unremitted balances to the concerned government agencies.

We recommend that the Municipality of San Guillermo ensure compliance with the statutory minimum net take-home pay as outlined in the General Appropriations Act (GAA), by closely monitoring the general payroll.

To further enhance the Municipality's commitment to gender equality and maximize the impact of the GAD Fund, the following recommendations were made:
Sustain GAD Fund Allocation:  Continue prioritizing at least 5% of the annual budget for GAD-related programs to ensure sustained support for gender equality initiatives and women’s empowerment.
Strengthen Monitoring and Evaluation: Establish more robust monitoring and evaluation systems to assess the effectiveness of GAD-funded projects, ensuring that goals are met, and resources are used efficiently.
We commended the management on its effort to support and properly implement priority programs and projects for the local risk reduction and management of the Municipality of San Guillermo.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:

The Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to prepare Annexes A to E, as assigned to each, together with proper and relevant supporting documents and submit the same to the Auditor in adherence with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

The Municipal Accountant should be required to prepare the CIP ledger cards and other supporting documents for all infrastructure projects and submit to the Resident Auditor further evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to maintain special accounts in the General Fund for the 20% Development Fund, including maintenance of complete subsidiary ledgers to achieve fair presentation of its accounts and transactions and for proper disclosures as intended by Sections 105 and 110, Volume I of NGAS and Section 313 of RA 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Schedule of all liabilities with Ageing of each payable to establish that all liabilities are presented in compliance with IPSAS 1, otherwise in the absence of such details and ageing and if found without basis for recognition in the books, derecognition of the liabilities may be warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to submit Liquidation Report together with the relevant supporting documents, including the related Memorandum of Agreement for each fund received for further evaluation in audit and in case of non-compliance, these funds should be returned immediately to the Source Agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Sangguniang Bayan require the creation of a separate depository account for the 20% Development Fund in compliance with Sections 310 and 313 of RA No. 7160 and Section 105, Volume I of NGAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to remit all the prior period unremitted balances to the concerned government agencies. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant confer with the concerned agencies to determine remedies that shall ensure that these long-outstanding balances be validated upon remittance/payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to justify the remittances in excess of amounts withheld and submit proper documents to establish the propriety and validity of the disbursements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balances of CY 2021 Calamity Funds as Special Trust Funds in the Trust Fund books in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer should also ensure that the Trust Liabilities and Cash in Bank accounts for DRRMF are reconciled.
We recommended that the management return the unutilized BGCM funds to the National Treasury in compliance with the DBM Circular Letter and comply with the posting and reporting requirements thereto.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to submit validated deposit slip showing the actual deposit of these cash on hand.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Accountable Officers to submit Acknowledgment Receipts for all donations in-kind, Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and furnish copies to the Municipal Accountant and the Auditor. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that proper recognition and disclosure of donations in-kind are made in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the management ensure that all the proper and relevant documents relative to payments of salaries and other form of compensation required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2021 and other pertinent rules and regulations are completely submitted together with the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the management ensure that payments for these allowances shall not exceed P5,000.00 and are supported with relevant and pertinent documents similarly required by COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish compliance with the conditions set under AO No. 36 dated November 16, 2020 and reiterated under DBM – DOH Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020 dated November 25, 2020.

We recommended that the management should ensure that the grant and liquidation of cash advances comply with the guidelines and are supported with complete documents in compliance with COA rules and regulations. It was also recommended that another permanent employee who is properly bonded be designated to handle specific cash advances that may assist and relieve the present AO with multiple cash advances.

observations.recommendation:

b) Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the appropriation for Personnel Services for one fiscal year shall not exceed the required 55% of the TIRS realized in the next preceding fiscal year pursuant to Section 325 (a) of RA 7160; and

c) Enjoin the key personnel to exercise prudence in the analysis and review of their respective reports and ensure faithful compliance with statutory and administrative requirements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer noted the observation and recommendation. This had been addressed upon discovery during the SGLG evaluation on the aspect of growth rates of collections. Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer committed to be more cautious in ensuring an accurate Statement of Receipts and Expenditures for the preparation of a compliant PS Cap in the succeeding Annual Budget by the Municipal Budget Officer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

a) Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that there are existing available appropriations for payments for PS expenses, otherwise no obligations should be authorized;

b) Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their reports regularly to address discrepancies early on and both should ensure that their financial and budget reports are prepared more accurately; and 

c) Enjoin all concerned officials to strictly observe all existing rules and regulations relative to the above observations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer noted the audit observation and recommendation on the Misclassification of account. The MBO just tried to separate Terminal Leave Benefits of the retired employees from that of the monetization of leave credits. Nevertheless, beginning FY 2020, the management will be using the Account Title for Terminal Leave benefits and payment of monetization of leave credits. Likewise, on the monetization of leave credits of 3 employees aggregating to P155,976.77, a separate fund augmentation was approved by the LCE and attached to their respective vouchers. Also, Section 4.5 of the Budget Ordinance No. 2018-8 prioritizes the use of Savings for payment of compensation, retirement gratuity and terminal leave benefits. Although, the amount of appropriation for terminal leave benefits is at that time, it is our understanding that we can augment funds from PS savings as implied in the provision of the Ordinance. Nevertheless, the COA recommendation to this matter is highly noted. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer committed to reconcile the variance and submit an accurate report and a regular reconciliation of their reports will be done this CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended Management to endeavor in improving their cash management, reassess their planning, budget execution and monitoring processes and assertively pursue feasible solutions that will resolve the possible cash deficit.

We recommended the following corrective actions:
The Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert back the unutilized LDRRMF of years 2013 and earlier years totaling P1,388,299.51 to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund for it to be available for other social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian;
The Municipal Accountant to establish accurately the CYs 2018 and 2019 unexpended balances for QRF and DRRMF-MOOE and the financial assistance, make the necessary adjusting entries both in the General Fund and Trust Fund books effecting the required transfer and observe similar procedure in the succeeding allocations; and
The concerned officials to strictly observe and comply with the reportorial requirements as prescribed by the above-cited rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the nature and details of the variances noted between the two reciprocal accounts, obtain documents to establish the balances of the accounts and make corresponding adjustments, if appropriate. Also, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that a periodic reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts is conducted in order to eliminate the variances.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Municipal Accountant to analyze and review the nature and details of the account balances and upon proper evaluation and analysis, ensure that remittances be made accordingly;
Direct the concerned Officers to obtain Business Policy Numbers of those newly hired employees from the GSIS as reference for the remittance of their contributions and other payments pertaining to them; and
Ensure that the inter-agency accounts are presented and disclosed in the financial statements at its fair and accurate balances.
We also recommended that the Human Resources and Management Officer to require and obtain the Business Policy Numbers of the newly hired employees and submit to the Municipal Accountant for the remittance of their contributions or any amounts withheld from them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to establish the accurate balances of the Due to NGAs accounts by providing detailed ledgers and supporting schedules. We also recommended that all the Department Heads concerned to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the submission of supporting documents relative to the receipt, implementation and liquidation of the fund transfers which are under their monitoring.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require that all collections should be deposited intact daily, if not, as regular as possible to avoid stocking up substantial amount of collections in the custody of the collecting officers. Further, Management should also give priority in providing means of transportation of the Municipal Treasurer or the Cashier to the Bank every time they need to transact business with the LGUs servicing and depository banks.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the outstanding loans account into the appropriate account, Due to National Government Agencies (Due to NGA) where the related transactions should be properly accounted consistent with the account description as provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that management undertake proper gender mainstreaming and attribution of GAD funds through the use of HGDG and PIMME to establish the appropriateness and validity of expenses to be charged out of GAD Fund. The management should also prepare the other required documents to be submitted together with the GAD Accomplishment Report as listed in the said JMC.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management should enforce settlement of current and outstanding cash advances from its officers and employees and for its dormant receivables, make a written request for write-off in compliance with COA rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Management submit all the required documents and justification for the promotion of more than three-grade step increment; otherwise, the appointment may be considered invalid and any payments of salaries and other benefits found to be irregular, illegal and excessive shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that there are existing available appropriations for payments for PS expenses, otherwise no obligations should be authorized;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their reports regularly to address discrepancies early on and both should ensure that their financial and budget reports are prepared more accurately; and
Enjoin all concerned officials to strictly observe all existing rules and regulations relative to the above observations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to be more cautious in ensuring that revenue and non-revenue receipts are reported in the proper accounts in order to derive an accurate Statement of Receipts and Expenditures that serves as basis of the Budget Officer in the computation of PS Limitation;
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the appropriation for Personnel Services for one fiscal year shall not exceed the required 55% of the TIRS realized in the next preceding fiscal year pursuant to Section 325 (a) of RA 7160; and
Enjoin the key personnel to exercise prudence in the analysis and review of their respective reports and ensure faithful compliance with statutory and administrative requirements.

We recommended that the Management:
Require comprehensive reconciliation of records between the Accounting and Budget Office early on in order that any differences in their reports will be properly addressed;
Stop charging ineligible expenses against the 20% Development Fund and ensure that the appropriation of project expenditures and/or utilization of Funds are strictly in accordance with the JMC; and
Adhere to the above laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management should ensure compliance with the conditions and review comments cited by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 on the utilization, programming and implementation of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Management submit the above-listed documents, which may be applicable for all the projects that were implemented in CY 2019 and those which construction or implementation are still on-going in order that an objective evaluation of the contract and proper inspection be undertaken.

We recommended that the Management ensure that deliveries of procured goods be inspected and validated by the audit team in compliance with the laws, rules and regulations. We further recommended that the resident auditor be notified within 24 hours from acceptance, the time, day and place of delivery to allow the auditor to confirm the conduct of inspection and validation.

We recommended that all the Accountable Officers prepare and submit a detailed and accurate documents and reports and ensure that the Certification are made in the Cashbooks in accordance with the above-rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that Financial Statements are prepared strictly in accordance with IPSAS 1 in order to achieve a more reliable, comparative and understandable financial reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to make the compulsory disclosures of LDRRM Funds in the Notes to Financial Statements in the Annual Financial Statements in compliance with IPSAS and COA Rules and further, to submit belatedly a revised CY 2019 Notes to Financial Statements.

2.delay in the collection of voucher
3.late closing of books
4. unreleased vouchers and released outside the office.

The management committed to consider all the issues to ensure early submissions of accounts for the year 2018.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management strictly adhere with COA Circular Nos. 74-34, 87-278 and 2009-001.  Copies of perfected contracts and Purchase Orders must be submitted to the Office of the Audit Team Leader for proper review and evaluation thereof.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to deduct from the salaries and/or other monetary claims of the concerned Municipal personnel the amount of disallowance due from them and strictly observe the provisions of Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2009- 006.  Records of the Disallowance should be properly maintained, updated and monitored.
observations.managementAction:
Management exerted all its efforts
to recover the  records/details of the amount of disallowances appearing in the books of the municipality but still failed to identify the person liable, thereby creating possibility of non-collection.

observations.recommendation: 1.4 We recommended that the Management comply with the minimum limit of P 3,500.00 for employee’s monthly net take home pay pursuant to Section 52 of GAA 2016. We also recommended that statutory authorized deductions shall be given preference over other deductions.

3.4 We recommended that the:
Collecting Officer  reconcile the cash balances with the accounting records on a monthly basis;
Management require the Collecting Officer to prepare Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms and to submit a copy to the Auditor’s Office for verification; and
Collecting Officer maintain permanent record book for accountable forms as prescribed.

5.7 We recommended that Management immediately effect the insurance of its properties with the Government Insurance Fund of GSIS in accordance with the said Administrative Order and COA Circular to avoid any conflict and legal responsibility as regards to the proper insurance of its properties and allowing the Municipality to be indemnified from possible loss of property that it may sustain.

6.7 We reiterated our audit recommendation that Management instruct the Inventory Team to finish the inventory taking of all the properties of the Municipality;  prepare and submit the result of the physical inventory that will be used by the designated Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant as basis for reconciling their records. In case the concerned employees failed to render the required reports and maintain property records, enforce Section 122 of the P.D. No. 1445 which states that “failure on the part of officials concerned to submit required documents and reports shall automatically cause the suspension of payment of their salaries until they shall have complied with the requirements”.
6.8 Moreover, we recommended that Management make the necessary adjustments to the PPE accounts after the physical inventory and reconciliation of the accounts in the books and after the provisions of the necessary depreciation to present reliable balances in the financial statements.

7.5 We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee Secretariat and the General Services Officer  submit copies of approved contracts, purchase order, together with supporting documents within five (5) days from execution and Notice of Deliveries within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance to the Audit Team to ensure timely review/evaluation of the contracts and inspection of deliveries, respectively.

8.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant substantiate disbursement of funds for services rendered by job order and service contract personnel with the necessary and appropriate supporting documents in accordance with the aforementioned rules and regulations to establish the validity and propriety of their claims for wages.

9.7 We recommended that Management comply with the following:
Submit the Monthly Report Of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption every end of the month
Ensure that all supporting documents especially the Driver’s Trip Tickets (DTT) are properly and completely accomplished and transmitted for audit; and
Establish and implement effective control measures in the use of government vehicle and fuel consumption as provided in COA Circular No. 77-61

10.8 We recommended that Management (a) provide for a motor pool wherein unserviceable properties as well as assets still use for operations could be properly secured and  safeguarded against the elements of wear and tear; (b) create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee who will be responsible for the evaluation of the reasonable resale value of the unserviceable properties listed in the Inventory and Inspection Report of  Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and the immediate disposal of said items; (c) promptly cause the sale of the unserviceable motor vehicles thru public auction in accordance COA Circular No. 86-264.

11.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the OIC- Municipal Treasurer cause the timely and full remittance of the total taxes withheld on or before the 10th day of the succeeding month, in conformity with BIR Regulations, thereby helping the national government in raising additional revenues.

12.7 We recommended that the Management  strictly comply with Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002, and ensure that  (a)  the cash advances of officials/employees are fully liquidated before granting another one; (b) make sure that said officials/employees settle their accounts within the time set by pertinent rules and regulations; and (c) exert all efforts to enforce the settlement of unliquidated cash advances especially to those who have long due cash advances related in prior years.

13.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant religiously comply with timely preparation and submission of monthly BRS for the Audit Team’s timely verification of the correctness of the Cash in Bank accounts. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare one (1) BRS for each bank account to identify easily the adjustments or reconciling items (if any) affecting a specific bank account.

15.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer transfer the remaining unexpended balance amounting to P 3,708,632.00 from the General Fund to a Special Trust Fund in compliance with the aforementioned provisions of R.A. No. 10121.

16.5 We recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare a certified monthly Report on the Utilization of DRRM Fund and submit to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month for the Audit Team’s on-time evaluation of the fund; and disclose the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that management require the conduct a complete inventory of the municipality’s PPE accounts and submit the required  Report of  Physical Count of Inventory of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) at least once a year.   Also, the General Services Office or the Property and Supply Management Division was recommended to maintain property ledger cards for each class of equipment. The RPCPPE should be reconciled with the equipment ledger cards, subsidiary ledgers and the balance in the general ledger to provide assurance that the account balances are fairly presented in the Financial Statements.

We also recommended that management adhere to the provisions of COA Circulars No. 2003-007 dated December 11, 2003 which took effect in January 2004, Circular Letter No. 2004-003 dated October 4, 2004 for guidance in computing the Depreciation Expense and COA Circular No. 2005-002 dated April 14, 2005, for the accounting policy on items with serviceable life of more than 1 year but small enough to be considered as PPE.

Moreover, we recommended that management make the necessary adjustments to the PPE accounts after the physical  inventory and reconciliation of the accounts in the books and after the provisions of the necessary depreciation to present a reliable balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management is currently implementing the prior year’s recommendation. During the year, management conducted actual physical count of PPE and agreed to adjust in the books subsequently upon completion of the report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management require the officials and employees of the municipality to settle outstanding cash advances and stop the grant of cash advances other than official travels.   Exert more effort to collect and compel accountable officers to settle their unliquidated cash advances. Management was also reminded that failure to settle the cash advances shall be a valid ground for the withholding of their salaries and application of sanctions as provided in the law.
observations.managementAction: Management introduced and implemented proper control in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances. Management is currently deducting the outstanding cash advances of retired Officials and employees to their terminal leave pay.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly bank reconciliation statements regularly and submit the same to the Office of the Audit Team Leader pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant is in the process of preparing the Bank reconciliation Statement as of December, 2016. Monthly BRS for the year 2016 were not submitted to the Audit team.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management closely monitor  the breeding stocks by the concerned officials and see to it that the provision in the Memorandum of Agreement for the program is being complied and adhered with to benefit other prospective beneficiaries.  Subsidiary ledgers/records should be maintained and regularly updated.  The concerned office should conduct inspection of the breeding animals for ascertaining the existence and recording of offspring to its proper account and evaluation during the turnover of the animals.
observations.managementAction: Management had implemented the prior year’s audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management properly support all the necessary documents for all disbursements on infrastructure projects as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the legality, propriety and validity of the disbursements.   See to it that subsequent claims / payments are properly documented.
observations.managementAction: Management rendered partial compliance on the aforementioned regulation due to the continued delayed/non-submission of all required supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management require  the municipal accountant and other responsible officials concerned to submit the revenue and disbursement documents, monthly trial balances, bank reconciliation statements and other required reports within the prescribed period to the Office of the Auditor for timely conduct of the required audit on the accounts and transactions of the municipality so that fairness of the presentation of the financial statements can be ascertained.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to comply on the timely rendition of accounts. Other reports were submitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management strictly adhere with COA Circular Nos. 74-34, 87-278 and 2009-001.  Copies of perfected contracts and Purchase Orders must be submitted to the Office of the Audit Team Leader for proper review and evaluation thereof.
observations.managementAction: Management did not submit the required copies of contracts and purchase orders within the prescribed period from its issuance, instead, the said required documents would only be seen by the Auditor when the disbursement vouchers and corresponding supporting documents were submitted for post- audit.
